text: Welcome to another installment of robotic hernia repairs. Today we're going to be talking about robotic tap ventral hernia repair with mesh.

This is a case in a fifty nine year old man with a history of prior laparoscopic cholecystectomy who then had an umbilical hernia related to a twelve millimeter trocar at the belly button. The patient presented to the ED multiple times with pain. His past medical and surgical history will otherwise unremarkable his physical exam. He had a BMI of thirty-four and an incarcerated hernia at the umbilicus. No other hernias.

He had a small diastasis of three to four centimeters, but was not bothered by this issue, so I felt that he was a great candidate for an RTAB ventral hernia repair. Imaging was an ultrasound, but I did not obtain any CT scans beforehand.

The surgeon places robotic trocars and begins the operation by reducing the incarcerated umbilical hernia, carefully separating adhesions of the omentum from the hernia sac. The procedure progresses to the development of the peritoneal flap.

The surgeon begins dissection in the falciform fat pad, moving approximately five centimeters away from the umbilicus to ensure broad mesh coverage. The dissection remains in the preperitoneal plane, avoiding entry into the retromuscular space.

The surgeon observes a favorable anatomy with a substantial amount of fat in the preperitoneal layer, facilitating a smooth and easy dissection. The development of the peritoneal flap is underway, progressing efficiently due to the presence of a well-defined preperitoneal fat pad.

Once you get comfortable with the tap approach, using that hand over hand paddling maneuver will give you an easy way of developing this flap. You have to be careful though, in patients with thinner flaps, that kind of a maneuver can result in large holes, which will,

The surgeon focuses on reducing a small hernia sac and then proceeds to dissect the main peri-umbilical hernia, carefully working to find and develop the dissection plane while removing excess fat tissue.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand to apply strong traction on the hernia sac, effectively pulling it down for reduction. A small hole in the peritoneum is visible, which will be addressed later.

The surgeon performs delicate dissection to reduce the hernia sac, carefully avoiding bleeding while identifying the correct plane to reduce the incarcerated pre-peritoneal fat. The procedure transitions to contralateral preperitoneal dissection, facilitated by the patient's straightforward anatomy.
The surgeon completes the dissection and reduction of the hernia sac, creating a broad pocket for mesh placement. The pre-cut mesh is prepared for a two-centimeter hernia.
The surgeon uses a V-Lock absorbable suture to close the defect in a transverse orientation, preparing the space for finalization after cutting the mesh to twelve centimeters.
The surgeon uses a DeBakey forceps to manipulate the umbilical stock, intermittently taking bites through it to ensure the preservation of the belly button's appearance by the end of the procedure.
The surgeon takes good bites of the fascia to close the defect, adjusting insufflation pressure between twelve and fifteen to optimize visualization during the fascial closure.
The surgeon identifies a small defect superior to the umbilical hernia, deciding not to close it to avoid unnecessary tension. The defect is left open, with plans for broad coverage using mesh.

The surgeon runs the V-lock suture backwards to lock it on the fascia, preparing to place the Bard Soft Mesh. The mesh is positioned with the rough side facing the abdominal wall to enhance adherence, minimizing the need for manipulation.

The surgeon ensures the pocket is appropriately sized for the mesh, centers it, and directs it cranially due to the patient's diastasis. Mesh fixation is performed using interrupted stitches with a 3-0 polysorb suture, cut to twenty centimeters, placed in the abdomen. Three to four stitches are used for fixation, with additional sutures available for closing any peritoneal holes.

The surgeon prepares for the procedure by placing a 20-centimeter polysorb suture, two six-inch V-lock sutures, and a mesh cut to size. The mesh is centrally fixed, and any free-hanging mesh edges are secured to prevent rolling or clamshelling after the peritoneal flap closure. Instrument exchanges are minimized, typically involving the use of a monopolar scissor and a mega suture cut.

The surgeon begins closure of the peritoneal flap and fenestrations, starting at one apex and sewing from left to right. The initial bites are challenging due to limited hand mobility, but the process becomes easier as the closure progresses centrally. A baseball stitch technique is used for the flap closure, with the suture line reversed at the end to lock the V-lock securely.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to close a small fenestration near the umbilical hernia, completing the final steps of the procedure.

Operation was great. Total console time was I think less than thirty minutes for this case. You see, operations, once you learn how to do them and get comfortable with them, can be very efficient. A lot of people when they're early in their learning curve can take quite a bit of time to perform these operations, but once you learn how to dissect in that preperitoneal space, you're able to get these done efficiently, and you're able to integrate higher volume case numbers for robotic tap ventral. Again, thirty-one minute total operative time, twenty-three minute console time, same day discharge, no narcotics used in a post-op period, great operation, a great option for patients with smaller defects.
